questions.prompt: How do you ask 'How old are you?' in Croatian (formal)?
questions.explanation: Koliko = how many. Imate = you have (formal). Godina = years (genitive plural).
Dvadeset = 20, tri = 3. Croatian numbers follow the pattern: tens + ones.
Brat = brother. Otac = father. Sin = son. Ujak/stric = uncle.

questions.prompt: 'Imam ___ brata.' — Which form follows a number 2-4?
questions.explanation: Dva is used with masculine nouns. Dvije is for feminine. Brat → brata (genitive singular after 2-4).
Pet = 5, -naest = -teen. Petnaest = 15. Pedeset = 50.
Baka = grandmother. Djed = grandfather. Teta = aunt. Majka/mama = mother.

questions.prompt: 'Imam trideset ___ godina.' — What comes after numbers 5+?
questions.hint: After 5+, the number stays in its base form and the noun takes genitive plural.
questions.explanation: Numbers 5+ use the base cardinal form. Godina stays in genitive plural.

questions.prompt: Imam dvadeset ___ godine. (one = jedna for feminine)
questions.hint: After 'imam', godina takes accusative. Jedna → jednu.
questions.explanation: 'Jednu godinu' — after 1, the noun is accusative singular. Jedna → jednu (fem acc).
Sestra = sister. Moja sestra = my sister.

questions.prompt: On ___ četrdeset godina. (to have, 3rd person singular)
questions.explanation: Ima = has (3rd person singular of imati).
Kći = daughter. After numbers 2-4, feminine nouns take genitive singular: kći → kćeri.

questions.prompt: Mi ___ dvoje djece. (to have, 1st person plural)
questions.explanation: Imamo = we have (1st person plural of imati).
Otac = father (formal). Tata = dad (informal).
Literally 'I have 25 years.' Croatian uses 'imati' (to have) for age, not 'biti' (to be).
Moja = my (fem). Obitelj = family. Velika = big (fem adjective).

questions.prompt: Imam jednog brata i dvije sestre.
questions.correctAnswer: I have one brother and two sisters.
acceptableAnswers: I have a brother and two sisters.
questions.explanation: Jednog brata = one brother (acc). Dvije sestre = two sisters (gen sg after 2-4).
Djed = grandfather. Sedamdeset = 70.
Koliko = how many. Imaš = you have (informal). Literally: 'How many years do you have?'

questions.hint: Croatian uses 'to have' not 'to be' for age.
questions.explanation: In Croatian you 'have' years: Imam + number + godina.
Majka/mama = mother. Učiteljica = teacher (female).

questions.hint: 'Dvoje' is used for mixed-gender groups or collective nouns like 'djeca'.
questions.explanation: Dvoje = two (collective). Djeca → djece (genitive). Dvoje djece = two children.
Tvoj = your (informal). Brat = brother. Word order is flexible in Croatian.
Četrdeset = 40, sedam = 7. Croatian numbers: tens first, then ones.

questions.prompt: Conjugate 'imati' (to have) in the present tense.
questions.correctAnswer: ja imam, ti imaš, on/ona/ono ima, mi imamo, vi imate, oni/one/ona imaju
questions.hint: This is a standard -am conjugation verb.
questions.explanation: Imati follows the -am pattern: -am, -aš, -a, -amo, -ate, -aju.
questions.exampleSentence: Imam veliku obitelj — tri brata i jednu sestru.
questions.exampleTranslation: I have a big family — three brothers and one sister.

questions.prompt: Conjugate 'voljeti' (to love) in the present tense.
questions.correctAnswer: ja volim, ti voliš, on/ona/ono voli, mi volimo, vi volite, oni/one/ona vole
questions.hint: Despite the -jeti ending, this conjugates as an -im verb.
questions.explanation: Voljeti → volim. Like živjeti, the -jeti infinitive becomes -im in the present.

questions.prompt: Conjugate the negative of 'imati' (to not have) in the present tense.
questions.correctAnswer: ja nemam, ti nemaš, on/ona/ono nema, mi nemamo, vi nemate, oni/one/ona nemaju
questions.hint: The negative prefix 'ne' merges with the verb.
questions.explanation: Ne + imam = nemam. The negative forms are written as one word, just like with 'biti'.
questions.exampleTranslation: I don't have any brothers or sisters.

questions.prompt: Conjugate 'znati' (to know) in the present tense.
questions.correctAnswer: ja znam, ti znaš, on/ona/ono zna, mi znamo, vi znate, oni/one/ona znaju
questions.hint: This is a standard -am conjugation verb.
questions.explanation: Znati follows the regular -am pattern: -am, -aš, -a, -amo, -ate, -aju.
questions.exampleTranslation: Do you know how old he/she is?

questions.prompt: Conjugate 'trebati' (to need) in the present tense.
questions.correctAnswer: ja trebam, ti trebaš, on/ona/ono treba, mi trebamo, vi trebate, oni/one/ona trebaju
questions.explanation: Trebati follows the -am pattern. It can also be used impersonally: 'treba mi' = I need.
